sourceTitle: The only Notion setup you need for keeping New Year’s resolutions

questions.question: What is the main purpose of conducting a yearly retrospective according to the guide?

options.text: To celebrate all achievements without examining areas for improvement
options.explanation: The guide emphasizes reflection to surface what’s most important, not just celebration.

options.text: To surface what’s most important to you and shape next year’s planning
options.explanation: A yearly retrospective helps you identify priorities and informs goal‑setting for the next year.

options.text: To create a detailed financial budget for the upcoming year
options.explanation: Financial budgeting is a possible outcome, but the guide frames the retrospective as a broader perspective‑gathering exercise.

options.text: To compare your performance with colleagues’ performance
options.explanation: The guide focuses on personal reflection, not external comparison.

questions.question: Which Notion property does the guide recommend for categorizing goals by life‑area (e.g., Health, Career)?

options.text: Relation
options.explanation: Relation links databases together; categorizing within one database uses a different property.

options.text: Select
options.explanation: A Select property lets you assign a predefined category such as "Health and Fitness" or "Career Growth".

options.text: Formula
options.explanation: Formulas calculate values; they don’t provide a simple drop‑down category.

options.text: Rollup
options.explanation: Rollups aggregate data from related items, not used for simple categorization.

questions.question: How does Marie keep her weekly planner organized in Notion?

options.text: By creating a separate page for each week and manually copying tasks each time
options.explanation: The guide describes an automated template, not manual copying.

options.text: Using a Notion gallery where each page is a week, with a linked view filtered to that week’s actions
options.explanation: Marie uses a weekly‑planner gallery and a linked database view filtered to the week’s actions.

options.text: Embedding a Google Calendar view that shows weekly events
options.explanation: The guide focuses on Notion databases, not external calendar embeds.

options.text: Creating a Kanban board and moving cards daily
options.explanation: Kanban is not mentioned for the weekly planner; the described method uses galleries and linked views.

questions.question: Which of the following lenses is NOT part of Steve Schlafman's five lenses for a yearly retrospective?

options.text: Success and growth
options.explanation: This is one of the five lenses listed in the guide.

options.text: Falling short
options.explanation: This is also one of the five lenses.

options.text: People and relationships
options.explanation: This lens is included in the guide.

options.text: Financial wealth
options.explanation: Financial wealth is not listed among the five lenses; the guide mentions Health and wellness instead.

questions.question: What format does the guide suggest for turning retrospective insights into high‑level goals?

options.text: "SMART" (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time‑bound) goals
options.explanation: While SMART is a common framework, the guide specifically mentions a different format.

options.text: "Stop doing, start doing, keep doing" format
options.explanation: Steve Schlafman recommends using the "stop/start/keep" format to translate reflections into goals.

options.text: A simple checklist of tasks
options.explanation: A checklist is too granular; the guide talks about high‑level goal formation.

options.text: A quarterly OKR (Objectives and Key Results) sheet
options.explanation: OKRs are not mentioned in this guide as the primary format.

questions.question: How are quarters and months organized in Notion according to the guide?

options.text: As separate Tables with date columns for each entry
options.explanation: The guide uses Galleries, not Tables, for quarters and months.

options.text: As separate Galleries, each page representing a quarter or a month
options.explanation: Each quarter and each month is its own page inside a Gallery database.

options.text: As sub‑pages inside a single Year page, without any relation properties
options.explanation: While sub‑pages exist, the guide emphasizes using Galleries and Relation properties.

options.text: Using a Calendar view that automatically groups entries by quarter
options.explanation: Calendars are not described for quarter/month organization in this guide.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Relation properties among goals, projects, and next actions?

options.text: They automatically calculate the total time needed for each goal
options.explanation: Relations link records; calculations require formulas or rollups.

options.text: They allow you to see connected tasks across databases in one place
options.explanation: Relations let you view how goals, projects, and actions are linked, providing a holistic view.

options.text: They replace the need for any Select or Multi‑Select properties
options.explanation: Select properties are still useful for categorization; relations serve a different purpose.

options.text: They automatically generate weekly review templates
options.explanation: Templates are created manually; relations only connect items.

questions.question: Which template is described as a metrics‑driven approach with habits, milestones, and goals?

options.text: Annual review template
options.explanation: The annual review focuses on reflection, not a metrics‑driven habit system.

options.text: 2022 dashboard template
options.explanation: The dashboard aggregates areas of life but isn’t labeled as metrics‑driven.

options.text: Year manifesto template
options.explanation: The guide calls the Year Manifesto a metrics‑driven approach with habits, milestones, and goals.

options.text: Weekly planner template
options.explanation: Weekly planner is for short‑term tasks, not the broader metrics‑driven yearly plan.

questions.question: Why does the guide suggest keeping a single top‑level planning page with full‑page databases for goals, projects, months, quarters, and years?

options.text: Because it eliminates the need for any other Notion pages, reducing clutter to a single page
options.explanation: While it centralizes information, other pages (e.g., weekly templates) are still useful.

options.text: Because it lets you click into any database and instantly see what’s happening and when, improving accountability and focus
options.explanation: A unified dashboard gives a quick snapshot of all linked data, helping you stay accountable.

options.text: Because Notion only allows one database per page, so you must combine them all together
options.explanation: Notion allows multiple databases on a page; the reason is strategic, not a technical limitation.

options.text: Because it automatically syncs with external calendar apps without any additional setup
options.explanation: Syncing with external calendars requires separate integrations; the guide does not claim automatic sync.

questions.question: If you wanted to view only the actions that are due this week, which combination of Notion features would you use based on the guide?

options.text: Create a linked database view of the actions table, apply a filter for “Due date is within this week”, and embed it in a weekly planner template
options.explanation: The guide describes using a linked view filtered to weekly actions inside a weekly planner template.

options.text: Duplicate the actions table, manually delete rows that aren’t due this week, and place the copy on the dashboard
options.explanation: Duplicating and manually deleting defeats the purpose of dynamic filtering.

options.text: Add a Formula property that returns ‘This Week’ for relevant rows, then sort the entire actions table by that formula
options.explanation: Formulas can label rows, but the guide’s recommended method uses a filtered linked view, not sorting.

options.text: Use a Calendar view and switch the view mode to ‘Week’, then export the view to a PDF
options.explanation: Exporting to PDF isn’t mentioned; the guide focuses on in‑app linked views for weekly focus.
